1: Hypēretai Christou kai oikonomoi mystēriōn Theou (servants of Christ and stewards of God's mysteries): two distinct role-metaphors. Hypēretēs was originally the under-rower on a trireme — the lowest rank of naval service, not a free professional but a functionary of the fleet. Oikonomos (steward/household manager): in the Greco-Roman household, the oikonomos was typically a trusted slave who managed the household's resources and represented the paterfamilias in business dealings. Paul applies both titles to himself — not patron-figures but functional servants of a higher authority.
2: Pistos (faithful, trustworthy) is the primary qualification for a steward: the oikonomos must not embezzle or misrepresent his master's resources. Paul applies this to apostolic stewardship: faithfulness to the gospel, not rhetorical brilliance or popularity, is the criterion of genuine apostolic ministry. The Corinthian evaluation of Paul by his rhetorical performance is applying the wrong criterion.
3: Eis elachiston estin (it is a very small thing to me): Paul's studied indifference to human evaluation — anthrōpinē hēmera (human day/court). The judicial language is deliberate: a hēmera (day) in Greco-Roman legal practice was the day of trial; the Corinthian evaluations of Paul are amateur tribunal sessions. His own self-evaluation is equally unreliable (oud' emauton anakrinō), since self-knowledge is also limited. Only the Lord's evaluation on the Day (1 Corinthians 4:5) is final.
4: Suneidōs (conscience, literally: shared knowledge with oneself): the conscience-concept in Greco-Roman ethics referred to the inner witness that evaluated one's own conduct. Seneca, Cicero, and the Stoics treated the conscience as the authoritative inner court. Paul does not dismiss the conscience — it witnesses that he is not aware of anything against him — but relativizes it: the conscience is not the final court; the Lord is. A clear conscience is not the same as divine acquittal.
5: Mē pro kairou ti krinete (judge nothing before the appointed time): the eschatological deferral of judgment. The final reckoning belongs to the Lord's Day, when ta krypta tou skotous (the hidden things of darkness) will be brought to light. In the context of Corinthian factional evaluation of apostles, Paul is saying: stop running your amateur tribunal on apostolic performance; God will evaluate properly at the Parousia. Epainos (praise) will then come to each person — the honor-verdict belongs to God to award, not to the community.
6: Metaschēmatizoō (applied, transferred): Paul acknowledges that the Paul-Apollos dispute is the specific instance of a general principle he is teaching in abstract form. By speaking of himself and Apollos as examples, he avoids personalizing the critique while making it applicable to whoever is actually causing the factions. To mē hyper ha gegraptai (nothing beyond what is written): possibly a principle that apostolic authority is bounded by Scripture — no apostle can claim authority beyond what the OT grounds.
7: Ti de echeis ho ouk elabes (what do you have that you did not receive?): Paul's fundamental challenge to Corinthian pride. In the Greco-Roman honor system, legitimate boasting required genuine achievements and inherited status — things that were genuinely yours. Paul's counter: everything you have was given, not achieved. The spiritual gifts, the wisdom, the eloquence — all received from God. Therefore all boasting is inappropriate; the language should be gratitude, not pride.
8: Ēdē kekoresmenoi este, ēdē eploutēsate, chōris hēmōn ebasileusate (already you are satisfied, already you are rich, already you have begun to reign — without us!): Paul's ironic portrait of Corinthian over-realized eschatology. The Corinthians appear to believe they have already entered the fullness of the eschatological kingdom — ēdē (already) is repeated three times for emphasis. The tradition of Stoic philosophers who claimed to be kings even in chains (Epictetus Diss. 1.1) may be in the background. Paul's sarcasm is pointed: if you have already reigned, then the suffering apostles are left behind.
9: The Roman triumphal procession (thriambos): a victorious general would parade through Rome with his army, his spoils, and at the very end, the conquered enemies destined for execution. Paul inverts this image: God has put the apostles on display at the end of the procession — as those condemned to death, a spectacle (theatron) to the whole cosmos (angels and humans). The theatron metaphor is specifically theatrical: Paul and his co-workers are actors in a cosmic drama in which their humiliation is the spectacle. The Corinthians want to be in the honored section of the procession; the apostolic reality is the opposite.
10: The catalogue of contrasts — fools/wise, weak/strong, dishonored/honored — systematically inverts the Corinthian self-perception. The Corinthians see themselves as wise in Christ, strong, honored; Paul describes himself as the fool, the weak, the dishonored. This is the cross-shaped reality of genuine apostolic ministry: it conforms to the pattern of the crucified Christ rather than to the Sophistic ideal of the powerful sage. The irony (hēmeis de atimoi, we are without honor) is that the dishonored apostles are the ones who actually constitute the community the Corinthians boast in.
11: The peristasis catalogue (list of hardships) — a literary form used by Stoic philosophers to demonstrate the sage's endurance and freedom from external circumstances (Seneca Ep. 85.26; Epictetus Diss. 1.24). Paul uses the form but inverts its purpose: rather than demonstrating invulnerability through endurance, his catalogue demonstrates the pattern of the cross. Gymnēteuomen kai kolaphizometha kai astatouemen (we go hungry, are struck, are homeless) — specific physical deprivations that contrast sharply with the Corinthian picture of already-reigning prosperity.
12: Kopiōmen ergazomenoi tais idiais chersin (we work hard with our own hands): Paul worked as a tentmaker (skēnopoios, Acts 18:3) — a manual laborer. In the Greco-Roman status hierarchy, manual labor was beneath the dignity of anyone with sufficient social standing to be a philosophical teacher; Sophists demanded fees for their rhetorical services, which was itself a status-marker. Paul's manual labor was both a practical financial decision and a deliberate status-inversion: the apostle of the crucified messiah works with his hands. The blessing/cursing, endurance/slander responses (1 Corinthians 4:12b-13a) echo Sermon on the Mount ethics (Matthew 5:44).
13: Peripsēma tou kosmou egenēthēmen, pantōn peripsēma (we have become the scum of the world, the garbage of the world): the most severe social degradation language in Paul's letters. Peripsēma (scum, filth) was the term for the residue scraped off a dirty surface, or for human sacrificial victims used to purify a community — the lowest possible social classification. Paul applies it to himself and his co-workers not as self-pity but as theological statement: the apostolic pattern conforming to the crucified Christ is the pattern of deliberate social downward-mobility.
14: Nouthetō (I warn/admonish): a stronger term than simple instruction; parental correction. Paul identifies his relationship as paternal: hōs tekna mou agapēta (as my beloved children). In the Greco-Roman context, the relationship between a philosopher and his disciples was often described in quasi-parental terms (Seneca Ep. 2.1), but Paul makes it specifically covenantal: he has fathered them in the faith (en Christō Iēsou egennēsa, 1 Corinthians 4:15). The parent-child model grounds the authority of his correction without invoking the competitive patron-client framework.
15: Paidagōgous (guardians, tutors): the paidagōgos in the Greco-Roman world was typically a trusted slave who accompanied a child to school and supervised their conduct and moral development — not the teacher but the moral custodian. Paul distinguishes his role as spiritual father from the role of paidagōgoi (which may refer to other teachers or apostolic figures): you may have many custodians but only one father. En Christō Iēsou dia tou euaggeliou egennēsa (I fathered you in Christ Jesus through the gospel): the gospel itself is the generative event, with Paul as the agent of that spiritual genesis.
16: Mimētai mou ginesthe (become imitators of me): a standard Greco-Roman educational principle — the student imitates the master as the most direct form of learning. In philosophical schools, a student would follow the master's way of life, not merely absorb doctrines. Paul invokes this convention but grounds it christologically (1 Corinthians 4:17: my ways in Christ). The call to imitate Paul is not ego-driven but pedagogical: Paul's cross-shaped life is the most direct curriculum for the cross-shaped community.
17: Timothy appears here as Paul's apostolic representative — an established pattern in Paul's mission practice (Philippians 2:19-24; 1 Thessalonians 3:2; Acts 19:22). Timothy's role is to remind them of Paul's ways in Christ as Paul teaches in every church — underscoring that the Corinthian situation is not unique: the same cross-shaped apostolic pattern applies universally, not just in Corinth. The universal practice claim (kathos pantachou en pasē ekklēsia didaskō) relativizes Corinthian exceptionalism.
18: Hōs mē erchomenou de mou pros hymas ephysiōthēsan tines (some of you have become arrogant as if I were not coming): phusiomenoi (puffed up) — the same diagnosis as 1:12 and the signature Corinthian problem. The logic: Paul's absence was interpreted as inability or unwillingness to return, and some took advantage of it to advance their own influence. This is the patron-client dynamic again: with the founding patron absent, local leaders competed to fill the vacuum.
19: Eleusomar de tachos pros hymas (I will come to you very soon): the apostolic authority Paul withheld from his rhetoric (1 Corinthians 2:1-5) he now asserts directly. Ean ho Kyrios thelēsē (if the Lord wills): the standard Jewish and early Christian qualification on plans (James 4:15). Gnōsomai ou ton logon tōn pephysiōmenōn alla tēn dynamin (I will find out not the speech of the arrogant ones but their power): Paul applies the logos/dynamis contrast (1 Corinthians 2:4) to the evaluation of the Corinthian leaders — rhetoric is not the criterion; Spirit-power is.
20: Ou gar en logō hē basileia tou Theou alla en dynamei (for the kingdom of God is not a matter of talk but of power): the kingdom-language summarizes the entire argument of 1 Corinthians 1-4. The kingdom is not a rhetorical construct built with Sophistic skill but a pneumatic reality demonstrated through the Spirit's transforming power. The Corinthians' preference for Apollos's eloquence is a preference for logos over dynamis — they have confused the vehicle with the reality.
21: En rhabdō elthō pros hymas ē en agapē pneumati te prautētos (shall I come with a rod of discipline or in love and a spirit of gentleness?): Paul frames his coming visit as a parental choice — the disciplining rod (rhabdos) or the nurturing parent's gentleness. The rhabdos was the father's instrument of household correction; also the rod of the school disciplinarian. Paul leaves the choice to the Corinthians: their response to his letter will determine which version of fatherly Paul arrives. The rhetorical pressure of the question is designed to produce reform before his arrival.
